title: Atoms and Elements
subtitle: Atoms, elements, compounds and mixtures; symbols, the periodic table and chemical reactions.

studyGuide.heading: Atoms and elements
studyGuide.html:
• An atom is the smallest particle of a substance.
  • An element is a substance made of only one type of atom and cannot be broken down into anything simpler (e.g. oxygen, gold, carbon).
  • There are about 118 elements, each with a chemical symbol of one or two letters — the first letter is always a capital (e.g. O, Na, Fe).
studyGuide.mnemonic: Element = Exactly one type of atom. The first symbol letter is always a Capital, the second always lower-case (Na, not NA).

studyGuide.heading: Some symbols to know
studyGuide.html:
• H = hydrogen, O = oxygen, C = carbon, N = nitrogen.
  • Na = sodium, K = potassium, Fe = iron, Cu = copper, Au = gold, Ag = silver.
  • Cl = chlorine, Ca = calcium, Mg = magnesium, He = helium.

studyGuide.heading: Compounds
studyGuide.html:
• A compound is two or more elements chemically joined (bonded) together.
  • It has different properties from the elements that made it (e.g. water from explosive hydrogen + reactive oxygen).
  • A compound can only be split up by a chemical reaction, not by physical methods like filtering.
  • A formula shows the elements and how many atoms of each, e.g. H₂O = 2 hydrogen + 1 oxygen; CO₂ = 1 carbon + 2 oxygen.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.t{font:bold 11px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.s{font:9px Nunito;fill:#6b7790}</style><circle cx='40' cy='45' r='16' fill='#5b8def'/><text class='t' x='40' y='49' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>O</text><circle cx='22' cy='30' r='10' fill='#e0524d'/><text class='t' x='22' y='34' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>H</text><circle cx='58' cy='30' r='10' fill='#e0524d'/><text class='t' x='58' y='34' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>H</text><text class='t' x='40' y='85' text-anchor='middle'>Water H₂O</text><text class='s' x='40' y='98' text-anchor='middle'>compound</text><circle cx='210' cy='45' r='16' fill='#444'/><text class='t' x='210' y='49' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>C</text><circle cx='180' cy='45' r='14' fill='#5b8def'/><text class='t' x='180' y='49' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>O</text><circle cx='240' cy='45' r='14' fill='#5b8def'/><text class='t' x='240' y='49' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>O</text><text class='t' x='210' y='85' text-anchor='middle'>Carbon dioxide CO₂</text><text class='s' x='210' y='98' text-anchor='middle'>compound</text></svg>
diagram.caption: A compound is elements chemically bonded in fixed ratios.

studyGuide.heading: Elements, compounds and mixtures
studyGuide.html:
• Element — one type of atom (e.g. O₂, Cu).
  • Compound — different atoms chemically bonded (e.g. H₂O, NaCl).
  • Mixture — substances together but not chemically joined; easily separated (e.g. air, salt water).

A compound is made in a chemical reaction and has new properties; a mixture keeps the properties of its parts and can be separated physically.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 125' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bx{fill:#f7f9fd;stroke:#1f2a44;stroke-width:2}.t{font:bold 11px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.a{fill:#5b8def}.b{fill:#e0524d}</style><rect class='bx' x='8' y='12' width='104' height='78'/><circle class='a' cx='35' cy='38' r='9'/><circle class='a' cx='62' cy='38' r='9'/><circle class='a' cx='88' cy='40' r='9'/><circle class='a' cx='42' cy='65' r='9'/><circle class='a' cx='72' cy='66' r='9'/><text class='t' x='60' y='106' text-anchor='middle'>Element</text><rect class='bx' x='128' y='12' width='104' height='78'/><circle class='a' cx='150' cy='40' r='9'/><circle class='b' cx='164' cy='40' r='7'/><circle class='a' cx='196' cy='38' r='9'/><circle class='b' cx='210' cy='38' r='7'/><circle class='a' cx='168' cy='68' r='9'/><circle class='b' cx='182' cy='68' r='7'/><text class='t' x='180' y='106' text-anchor='middle'>Compound</text><rect class='bx' x='248' y='12' width='104' height='78'/><circle class='a' cx='270' cy='36' r='9'/><circle class='a' cx='300' cy='40' r='9'/><circle class='b' cx='314' cy='40' r='7'/><circle class='b' cx='272' cy='66' r='7'/><circle class='a' cx='332' cy='66' r='9'/><text class='t' x='300' y='106' text-anchor='middle'>Mixture</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Element = one kind of atom. Compound = different atoms bonded together. Mixture = not joined, easily separated.

studyGuide.heading: The periodic table
studyGuide.html:
• The periodic table lists all the elements in order.
  • Metals are on the left and middle; non-metals are on the right.
  • A group is a vertical column — elements in the same group have similar properties.
  • A period is a horizontal row.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 370 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.t{font:bold 12px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.s{font:bold 10px Nunito;fill:#2b9d6b}.b{font:bold 10px Nunito;fill:#3f6fd1}.tiny{font:9px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}</style><line x1='34' y1='22' x2='332' y2='22' stroke='#2b9d6b' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='332,22 324,18 324,26' fill='#2b9d6b'/><text class='s' x='110' y='15'>Period → (across a row)</text><line x1='22' y1='34' x2='22' y2='126' stroke='#3f6fd1' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='22,126 18,118 26,118' fill='#3f6fd1'/><text class='b' x='2' y='146'>Group ↓ (down a column)</text><rect x='34' y='32' width='216' height='94' fill='#d6e4fb' stroke='#1f2a44' stroke-width='2'/><text class='t' x='142' y='76' text-anchor='middle'>METALS</text><text class='tiny' x='142' y='92' text-anchor='middle'>(left and middle)</text><rect x='250' y='32' width='90' height='94' fill='#fde3c8' stroke='#1f2a44' stroke-width='2'/><text class='t' x='295' y='72' text-anchor='middle'>NON-</text><text class='t' x='295' y='88' text-anchor='middle'>METALS</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Metals fill the left and middle; non-metals are on the right. Columns = groups, rows = periods.

studyGuide.mnemonic: Groups go down (columns), periods go across (rows). Metals left, non-metals right.

studyGuide.heading: Properties of metals and non-metals
studyGuide.html:
• Metals: shiny, good conductors of heat and electricity, malleable (bend without breaking), strong, high melting points, mostly solid.
  • Non-metals: often dull, poor conductors (insulators), brittle when solid, lower melting points, many are gases.

studyGuide.heading: Chemical reactions and word equations
studyGuide.html:
• In a chemical reaction, reactants turn into products with new properties. A reaction is usually hard to reverse.
  • Mass is conserved — atoms are rearranged, not created or destroyed.
  • A word equation shows it: e.g. magnesium + oxygen → magnesium oxide.
  • Signs of a reaction: colour change, gas/bubbles, temperature change, a new solid (precipitate).

flashcards.back: The smallest particle of a substance.
A substance made of only one type of atom; cannot be broken down further.
One or two letters; first letter always capital, second always lower-case (e.g. Na).
Two or more elements chemically joined (bonded) together.

flashcards.front: How is a compound different from its elements?
flashcards.back: It has new, different properties and can only be split by a chemical reaction.
Each molecule has 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom.
Substances together but NOT chemically joined; easily separated physically.

flashcards.front: Where are metals on the periodic table?

flashcards.back: A vertical column; elements in it have similar properties.
A horizontal row of the periodic table.
Shiny, good conductors, malleable (also strong, high melting points).
Reactants are what you start with; products are what is formed.

flashcards.front: Is mass conserved in a reaction?
flashcards.back: Yes — atoms are rearranged, not created or destroyed.
Colour change, gas/bubbles, temperature change, or a new solid (precipitate).

commonMistakes.title: Writing symbols with two capitals
commonMistakes.detail: Only the first letter is a capital: Na not NA, Fe not FE. CO and Co mean different things.

commonMistakes.title: Confusing compounds and mixtures
commonMistakes.detail: Compound = chemically bonded, new properties, hard to separate. Mixture = not bonded, easily separated.

commonMistakes.title: Saying an element can be broken down
commonMistakes.detail: An element is made of one type of atom and cannot be broken into anything simpler chemically.

commonMistakes.title: Misreading a formula
commonMistakes.detail: In CO₂ the small 2 means 2 oxygen atoms joined to 1 carbon — not 2 whole molecules.

commonMistakes.title: Thinking mass increases when something burns
commonMistakes.detail: Mass is conserved; if it seems to change, a gas was taken in or given off (count all reactants/products).

questions.q: An element is a substance made of…
questions.explanation: An element contains only one type of atom.
questions.hint: One type of atom only.

questions.q: What is the chemical symbol for sodium?
questions.hint: It comes from its Latin name natrium.

questions.q: Which is a compound?
questions.explanation: Water is two elements (hydrogen and oxygen) chemically joined — a compound.
questions.hint: Which has more than one type of atom bonded?

questions.q: How many atoms are in one molecule of CO₂?
questions.hint: Count the carbon and the oxygens.

questions.q: Metals are found where on the periodic table?
questions.explanation: Metals are on the left and middle; non-metals on the right.
questions.hint: Non-metals are on the right.

questions.q: Which is a property of most metals?
questions.explanation: Metals are good conductors of heat and electricity.
questions.hint: Think about why wires are metal.

questions.q: State the difference between an element and a compound.
questions.modelAnswer: An element is made of only one type of atom and cannot be broken down into anything simpler. A compound is made of two or more different elements chemically joined together and can be broken down by a chemical reaction.
questions.hint: One type of atom vs different atoms bonded.
questions.examMistake: Saying a compound is just a mixture — it is chemically bonded.

questions.q: Why is the symbol for cobalt written 'Co' and not 'CO'?
questions.modelAnswer: A chemical symbol has a capital first letter and a lower-case second letter, so cobalt is Co. 'CO' would mean carbon (C) joined to oxygen (O) — a compound (carbon monoxide), which is completely different.
questions.hint: What would two capitals C and O mean?
questions.examMistake: Writing element symbols in all capitals.

questions.q: A vertical column of the periodic table is called a…
questions.explanation: A vertical column is a group; elements in it have similar properties.
questions.hint: Columns go down.

questions.q: Which is a mixture?
questions.explanation: Air is a mixture of gases not chemically joined.
questions.hint: Which is not chemically bonded?

questions.q: In a chemical reaction, what are 'reactants' and 'products'?
questions.modelAnswer: Reactants are the substances you start with that react together. Products are the new substances that are formed by the reaction.
questions.hint: Start with → end with.

questions.q: Complete the word equation: magnesium + oxygen → …
questions.explanation: Magnesium reacts with oxygen to form magnesium oxide.
questions.hint: Metal + oxygen → metal oxide.

questions.q: Explain why water is a compound and not a mixture of hydrogen and oxygen.
questions.modelAnswer: In water the hydrogen and oxygen atoms are chemically bonded together in a fixed ratio (2 hydrogen to 1 oxygen, H₂O), forming a new substance with completely different properties from the elements — water is a harmless liquid, while hydrogen and oxygen are gases. A mixture would not be bonded and could be separated physically, but water can only be split by a chemical process (e.g. electrolysis).
questions.hint: Think bonding, fixed ratio, and new properties.
questions.examMistake: Saying water is 'hydrogen and oxygen mixed' — they are bonded, not mixed.

questions.q: How many atoms in total are in one molecule of H₂SO₄?
questions.hint: Add up H(2), S(1), O(4).

questions.q: Give three ways the properties of metals differ from non-metals.
questions.modelAnswer: Metals are usually shiny while non-metals are often dull. Metals are good conductors of heat and electricity, whereas non-metals are usually poor conductors (insulators). Metals are malleable and can be bent or hammered into shape, while solid non-metals are brittle and snap. (Metals also generally have higher melting points and most are solid at room temperature.)
questions.hint: Appearance, conductivity, and what happens when you bend them.
questions.examMistake: Giving only one property or vague answers like 'metals are hard'.

questions.q: Why do elements in the same group of the periodic table behave similarly?
options: They have similar properties because of their position in the same column
questions.explanation: Elements in a group (column) share similar chemical properties.
questions.hint: Groups are the columns with shared behaviour.

questions.q: Iron filings and sulfur powder are mixed, then heated until they react. Explain how the mixture differs from the compound formed.
questions.modelAnswer: Before heating, the iron and sulfur are just a mixture: the iron can still be pulled out with a magnet and each keeps its own properties because they are not joined. When heated they react chemically to form iron sulfide, a new compound with different properties — it is not magnetic and cannot be separated by physical means, only by a chemical reaction.
questions.hint: Think about the magnet test before and after.
questions.examMistake: Not explaining that the compound has new properties and can't be separated physically.

questions.q: Which list contains only elements?
questions.explanation: Oxygen, iron and carbon are each made of only one type of atom — all elements.
questions.hint: Which contains no compounds or mixtures?

questions.q: A piece of magnesium is burned in air and the white powder formed weighs more than the original magnesium. Explain why mass is still conserved.
questions.modelAnswer: Mass is conserved because no atoms are created or destroyed — they are only rearranged. The magnesium reacts with oxygen from the air to form magnesium oxide, so the extra mass comes from the oxygen atoms that have joined the magnesium. If you included the mass of the oxygen taken from the air, the total mass before and after would be exactly equal.
questions.hint: Where did the extra mass come from?
questions.examMistake: Thinking burning creates mass — it gains oxygen from the air.

questions.q: Which observation is NOT a reliable sign that a chemical reaction has happened?
questions.explanation: Stirring is a physical action; colour change, gas and temperature change indicate reactions.
questions.hint: Which one is just a physical action?

questions.q: Write a word equation for the reaction of carbon burning in oxygen, and name the type of product.
questions.modelAnswer: carbon + oxygen → carbon dioxide. The product, carbon dioxide, is a compound (and an oxide, because a non-metal has reacted with oxygen).
questions.hint: Non-metal + oxygen → non-metal oxide.
questions.examMistake: Writing 'carbon monoxide' for complete burning in plenty of oxygen.

questions.q: The formula CaCO₃ contains how many oxygen atoms?
questions.explanation: The small 3 applies to the oxygen, giving 3 oxygen atoms.
questions.hint: Look at the subscript after the O.

questions.q: Classify each as element, compound or mixture and justify: (a) oxygen gas O₂, (b) carbon dioxide CO₂, (c) air.
questions.modelAnswer: (a) Oxygen O₂ is an element because it contains only one type of atom (oxygen), even though two are joined. (b) Carbon dioxide CO₂ is a compound because it is two different elements (carbon and oxygen) chemically bonded in a fixed ratio. (c) Air is a mixture because it contains several different substances (nitrogen, oxygen, carbon dioxide, etc.) that are not chemically joined and could be separated physically.
questions.hint: One atom type = element; bonded different atoms = compound; unbonded = mixture.
questions.examMistake: Calling O₂ a compound because it has two atoms — they are the same element.

questions.q: Explain why the properties of a compound such as sodium chloride (table salt) are so different from the elements sodium and chlorine.
questions.modelAnswer: Sodium is a soft, very reactive metal that fizzes violently in water, and chlorine is a poisonous green gas. When they react chemically, their atoms bond together to form sodium chloride, a completely new substance with its own properties — a stable, white, edible crystal that dissolves safely in water. The chemical bonding rearranges the atoms so the compound behaves nothing like either element on its own.
questions.hint: Contrast the dangerous elements with the safe compound.
questions.examMistake: Assuming a compound keeps the properties of its elements — it doesn't.

questions.q: In the reaction iron + sulfur → iron sulfide, 7 g of iron reacts completely with 4 g of sulfur. What mass of iron sulfide forms?
questions.explanation: Mass is conserved: 7 g + 4 g = 11 g of iron sulfide.
questions.hint: Atoms aren't lost — add the masses.

questions.q: Describe how you could decide whether a clear, colourless liquid is an element, a compound or a mixture.
questions.modelAnswer: First check if it can be separated by a physical method such as distillation or evaporation: if something is left behind or different liquids come off at different temperatures, it is a mixture. If it stays pure (boils at one fixed temperature) it is a pure substance. To tell if that pure substance is an element or compound, try to break it down chemically (e.g. by electrolysis): a compound can be split into simpler substances, but an element cannot be broken down into anything simpler.
questions.hint: Try physical separation first, then a chemical break-down.
questions.examMistake: Not distinguishing the physical test (mixture) from the chemical test (element vs compound).

questions.q: Magnesium ribbon is heated in a crucible with a loose lid. Explain why the lid is lifted occasionally and why this matters for measuring mass change.
questions.modelAnswer: The lid is lifted occasionally to let oxygen from the air get in so the magnesium can keep reacting to form magnesium oxide, but it is kept mostly on so that the white magnesium oxide smoke does not escape. This matters because if the oxide smoke escaped, you would lose some product and wrongly measure a smaller mass, hiding the true mass increase caused by oxygen joining the magnesium. Keeping the product in lets you show that the gain in mass equals the mass of oxygen added.
questions.hint: Balance letting oxygen in against losing the white smoke.
questions.examMistake: Not realising that escaping oxide smoke would falsify the mass measurement.

questions.q: Why can a compound NOT be separated by filtration or evaporation, but a mixture often can?
options: In a compound the elements are chemically bonded, so only a chemical reaction can separate them
questions.explanation: Chemical bonds in a compound require a chemical reaction to break, unlike the loose association in a mixture.
questions.hint: What holds a compound together?

questions.q: Use the periodic table layout to explain how you could predict whether an unknown element is likely to be a good conductor that can be hammered into shape.
questions.modelAnswer: If the element is on the left or middle of the periodic table, it is a metal. Metals are good conductors of heat and electricity and are malleable, meaning they can be hammered or bent into shape without shattering. So by checking the element's position — left/middle means metal — you can predict it is likely to be a good conductor and malleable, whereas an element on the right would be a non-metal and probably a brittle, poor conductor.
questions.hint: Position tells you metal vs non-metal, which tells you the properties.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that the prediction depends on metal/non-metal position.

questions.q: The formula for ammonium nitrate is NH₄NO₃. Work out the total number of atoms of each element and the grand total.
questions.modelAnswer: Nitrogen (N): there is 1 in NH₄ and 1 in NO₃, giving 2 nitrogen atoms. Hydrogen (H): the 4 in NH₄ gives 4 hydrogen atoms. Oxygen (O): the 3 in NO₃ gives 3 oxygen atoms. So there are 2 N + 4 H + 3 O = 9 atoms in total.
questions.hint: Count N, H and O across both parts of the formula.
questions.examMistake: Missing the second nitrogen in the NO₃ part.
